answer:
**Iqbal's concept of *khudi* (selfhood)—central to his later philosophy of dynamic ego-realization, self-affirmation, and striving beyond imitation toward infinite potential—is nascent but traceable in *Bang-e-Dara* through poetic motifs of the "self" as a force to discover, purify, and expand, rather than a explicit doctrinal term (absent in English translations).**[^1][^2]

### Part 1: Awakening and Purification of the Self
Early in the collection, *khudi* emerges as rejecting blind imitation (*taqlid*) in favor of authentic self-discovery and annihilation of the false ego. In "Majnoon ne sheher chhora," Iqbal urges: "Better to kill the self than walk in blind imitation's way; / Seek out your own true path, and your obsession with Khizr—abandon it." Here, "kill the self" evokes *fana* (ego-death) to birth true *khudi*, prioritizing personal vision over borrowed paths.[^3] Similarly, in the subsequent poem, the speaker declares: "I wander, searching for my own self, O Iqbal; / For I am the traveler, and the destination I am." Selfhood is both quest and unity, the self as origin and goal.[^4] In "Subah Ka Sitara," true life defies death's limits, implying *khudi*'s eternal aspiration: "True life is that which knows not Death's decree; / What life is this, where Death demands its fee?"[^5]

### Part 2: The Enigma of Human Selfhood
*Khudi* appears as restless inquiry into human essence. "Insaan" portrays Man as "an Enigma apart," thirsting for knowledge amid cosmic motion: "He is restless with a thirst for knowing, / But the mystery of life will not unfold." Unlike stars or sun bound to fate, humanity's self bears unique sorrow and autonomy: "There is no one to share the sorrow of Man; / How bitter is the existence of Man!" This isolates *khudi* as self-aware striving amid isolation.[^6]

### Part 3: Expansive Power of the Human Self
The theme culminates in human potential as conquering force. In "Insaan," unlike passive nature, "Every power of the Human Being is aflame with striving": "This single particle forever holds a yearning for vastness; / This is not a particle—perhaps it is a desert contracted to a point. / If it so chooses, it can change the very form of the garden; / This being is wise, is perceptive, is potent." *Khudi* is thus a compressed infinity, capable of remaking reality.[^7] Echoed in "Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam," youth must reclaim ancestors' action-oriented spirit over static talk.[^8] In "Shikwa," God's "timeless Self" contrasts human revival, implying communal *khudi* for Muslim renaissance.[^9]

**Overall, across parts, *khudi* evolves from purifying the self (Part 1), to its enigmatic restlessness (Part 2), to empowered vastness (Part 3)—foreshadowing Iqbal's mature view without prose exposition. No single definition paragraph exists; it's poetic intuition. (Corpus covers *Bang-e-Dara* fully but lacks later prose like *Asrar-e-Khudi*.)**[^10]

[^1]: No direct "khudi" or "selfhood" in English bodies; concept inferred from "self" + context (searches for "khudi," "selfhood," "ego" yielded 0 in Iqbal; "self" traces themes).
[^2]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, [work wiki](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/wiki/_work.card.md).
[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch.9, [p-e3d084](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/09-majnoon-ne-sheher-chhora-to-sehra-bhi-chhor-de/translation/#p-e3d084).
[^4]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch.11, [p-0db3d6](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/11-sakhtiyan-karta-hoon-dil-par-ghair-se-ghafil-hoon-/translation/#p-0db3d6).
[^5]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch.42, [p-e76893](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/42-subah-ka-sitara/translation/#p-e76893).
[^6]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch.14, [p-4ea800](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/14-insaan/translation/#p-4ea800).
[^7]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.12, [p-2697a9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/12-insaan/translation/#p-2697a9).
[^8]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.13, [p-365462](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462).
[^9]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.20, [p-ed83b1](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/20-shikwa/translation/#p-ed83b1).
[^10]: Corpus metadata confirms 37 works; *Bang-e-Dara* Parts 1-3 complete, no *Asrar-e-Khudi*.
